shower.thought: We spend decades learning how to be ourselves, then spend the rest of our lives unlearning it.
The people who hurt us most are usually just playing out a script written by someone who hurt them.
You can't remember most of your life, so technically you're living with an unreliable narrator inside your own head.
Nostalgia is just your brain editing out the boredom to keep the highlights reel.
The moment you admit you don't know something is the moment you actually start learning, which means most of education is just training people to pretend.
We call it "finding yourself" but we're usually just finding the version of ourselves that other people will accept.
Time moves slower when you're watching it, which means the worst moments of your life get extended on purpose while the good ones vanish.
Your phone knows you better than you know yourself, which is either terrifying or the most honest relationship you have.
We fear dying before we've lived, but we spend most of our actual life not living anyway.
The things you're most ashamed of are probably things everyone else has done but nobody talks about.
Confidence is just the ability to act like you know what you're doing long enough that other people believe it too.
If you had to describe your life to someone, you'd probably leave out the parts that actually defined it.
The older you get, the less you understand, but you're allowed to pretend more convincingly.
We assume success comes from doing more, but every major breakthrough is usually someone stopping doing something.
You're never really talking to other people—you're just talking to the version of them you've created in your head.
Forgetting something doesn't make it less real; it just means it happened to someone you used to be.
The most important people in your life are probably aware they're important, which somehow makes it worse.
We call it "growing up" but it's really just learning which disappointments are normal enough to accept.
Your biggest regrets are usually things you didn't do, which means fear is more expensive than failure.
Every decision you make is based on incomplete information, so technically you're always just gambling with your life.

motivational.thought: The master has failed more times than the beginner has even tried.
Your only competition is the version of yourself from yesterday.
Comfort is the enemy of growth, and growth is the only real comfort.
You miss 100% of the shots you never take.
— Wayne Gretzky
The cave you fear to enter holds the treasure you seek.
— Joseph Campbell
Small daily improvements are the antidote to overwhelming change.
Your circumstances don't determine your destination; your choices do.
The person who says it cannot be done should not interrupt the person doing it.
— Chinese Proverb
Discipline is choosing between what you want now and what you want most.
The only permission you need is your own.
Everything you want is on the other side of fear.
— Jack Canfield
Success is not final, failure is not fatal: it is the courage to continue that counts.
— Winston Churchill
You don't rise to the level of your goals; you fall to the level of your systems.
— James Clear
The biggest risk is not taking one.

darkjoke.thought:
I told my friend he had a drinking problem.
He said, "No I don't," and fell off the barstool for the third time that hour.

My dad said he wanted to be cremated when he died.
I said, "Dad, you're alive."
He said, "I know. I'm trying to hint."

I asked my dentist if the procedure would hurt.
He said, "Only when you see the bill."

My girlfriend said she was leaving me because I'm too pessimistic.
I said, "Good. I didn't think this would last anyway."

I went to a motivational speaker who said, "You can be anything you want!"
So far I've been unemployed, single, and deeply in debt.
He was right. The range is incredible.

My therapist asked if I had any suicidal thoughts.
I said, "Only on days ending in Y."
She started writing faster.

I told my mom I was thinking about dropping out of college.
She said, "Don't be ridiculous. You're already a disappointment—at least have a degree to show for it."

My ex-wife keeps texting me photos of our kids.
I keep telling her I have the originals.

I asked the waiter why the restaurant was closing.
He said, "Bad management, bad luck, and apparently one too many health code violations."
I said, "So you'll be open tomorrow?"
He said, "God willing."

I went to my accountant and asked if I could claim my emotional damage as a dependent.
He said, "That's not how taxes work."
I said, "That's not how my life works either, but here we are."

My son asked me if I was proud of him.
I said, "Ask me again when you've accomplished something."
He's twelve.

I told my wife she was being paranoid about me cheating.
She said, "Am I?"
The fact that it wasn't a question concerned me.

My doctor said I needed to reduce my stress.
I said, "Any suggestions?"
He said, "Well, you could stop coming to these appointments."

I applied for life insurance and they asked about my family history.
I said, "Short-lived and disappointed."
The agent put down his pen.

I told my barber I wanted to look younger.
He said, "You should've come in sooner."

My landlord asked why I was late on rent again.
I said, "I've been really busy."
He said, "Busy avoiding me?"
I said, "You're not invited."
He didn't laugh. He's still not laughing. I've been evicted.

I asked my boss what my five-year plan should be.
He said, "Don't have one. You won't be here in five years."
I said, "Wow, that's dark."
He said, "No, that's just what happens to everyone in this job."

My friend said he was cutting people out of his life who were toxic.
I said, "Good idea."
He said, "You start on Monday."

I went to a fortune teller who said, "I see great things in your future."
I said, "Really?"
She said, "No, I see your wallet. Hand it over."

I told my nutritionist I wanted to live longer.
She said, "Eat better, exercise more, reduce stress."
I said, "So basically give up everything I enjoy."
She said, "Now you're getting it."
